coredata.dc:title: The effects of rose pigments extracted by different methods on the optical properties of carbon quantum dots and its efficacy in the determination of Diazinon
coredata.dc:creator: Shekarbeygi, Zahra
Farhadian, Negin
Khani, Solmaz
Moradi, Sajad
Shahlaei, Mohsen
coredata.dc:description: This study examined the effect of two variables of rose pigments, and their extraction methods on optical properties of their synthesized carbon quantum dots. Afterward, a sensitive nanosensor based on fluorescence quenching of as prepared carbon dots is optimized for determination of Diazinon. The results indicated that the yellowish rose compounds obtained from alcoholic extraction are more stable and has higher quantum yield than others. Also the facile and reliable determination method has proved its ability for assessing the Diazinon in laboratory conditions as well as in real samples. The fluorescence decrement of carbon dots in the presence of Diazinon exhibits a linear relationship within the Diazinon concentration range of 0.02 to 1 µM and the relative standard deviation (RSD) for the detection of 0.01 μM of Diazinon (n = 3) is 3.5%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nano sensor
Carbon dots
Diazinon detection
Hydrothermal treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Common fluorescence measurements to the detection of Diazinon
Optimization of fluorescence response of CDs to Diazinon

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calibration curve. (A) The change fluorescence of the CDs against the increase of concentration of Diazinon. (B) A linear correlation of (F0-F) values to the concentration of Diazinon on the range from 0.02 to 1 µM.
The graphical of green synthesis of Cds and quenching the FL intensity in the presence of Diazinon.
The average size of CDs synthesized by hydrothermal treatment and fluorescence quantum yields for each of the CDs.
Determination of Diazinon in real samples.
List of different methods for determine Diazinon.

head.ce:title: The effects of rose pigments extracted by different methods on the optical properties of carbon quantum dots and its efficacy in the determination of Diazinon

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nano Drug Delivery Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Substances Abuse Prevention Research Center, Health Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Medical Biology Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Medical Biology Research Center, Health Technology Institute, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran (M. Shahlaei).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study examined the effect of two variables of rose pigments, and their extraction methods on optical properties of their synthesized carbon quantum dots. Afterward, a sensitive nanosensor based on fluorescence quenching of as prepared carbon dots is optimized for determination of Diazinon. The results indicated that the yellowish rose compounds obtained from alcoholic extraction are more stable and has higher quantum yield than others. Also the facile and reliable determination method has proved its ability for assessing the Diazinon in laboratory conditions as well as in real samples. The fluorescence decrement of carbon dots in the presence of Diazinon exhibits a linear relationship within the Diazinon concentration range of 0.02 to 1 µM and the relative standard deviation (RSD) for the detection of 0.01 μM of Diazinon (n = 3) is 3.5%.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus compounds [1] are generally consumed as pesticides and herbicides in agriculture as well as using in human wars as chemical weapons [2,3]. Their good effectiveness against the pests have gained the popularity of applying these compounds rather than those of Organochlorine and Carbamates [4]. The OP chemicals mostly inhibit acetyl cholinesterase (AChE) enzymes, thus causing permanent damage to the nervous system [5–7].

Diazinon (o,o-diethyl-O-(2-isopropyl-6-methylpyrimidin-4-yl)-monothiophosphate), is one of the most useful organophosphate pesticides which is widely used in agriculture and control of urban pests [8]. As mentioned, this chemical affects the activity of insect acetyl cholinesterase, and can also affect the human nervous system. Abnormal accumulation (AChE) in the synaptic gap in muscarinic, nicotinic and also central sites causes many tolls such as headache, seizure, and depression [9,10]. Exploit Diazinon at high doses cause diseases such as nausea, blurred vision, muscle thrombosis, severe breathing and death [11–13]. Another important worries about the usage of these insecticides is the pollution of surface water and its impact on aquatic organisms [14]. These make it important to develop fast, easy to use and high sensitive sensor to detect this chemical in low doses. Adjusting Diazinon concentration and monitoring its size is essential to reduce toxicity and accumulate in living organisms [15]. Unfortunately, there is no any standard tolerance range of existing Diazinon in drinking water but EPA US determined the maximum amount of Diazinon in healthy drinking water as 0.001 mg /L [16].

General and customary analytical techniques such as chromatography (HPLC, HPTLC, LC / MS, GC, GC / MS) [1,17–23], FT-Raman spectroscopy [24], immunoassays [25–27], biosensor [28] and electrochemical sensors [29–31] are used to evaluate and determine residues of pesticides. The techniques however are very sensitive and reliable, some disadvantages such as high costs, expensive equipment, the need for sample preparation and time-consuming of these methods have led to the demand for quick, simple, reasonable and cost-effective methods. Because of high sensitivity, low cost and speed along with operational simplicity, fluorescence base sensing techniques have attracted much attention in recent years. However, effective fluorescent materials such as Quantum Dots suffer from disadvantages including long preparation methods and high toxicity. At the moment carbon-based fluorescence nanoparticles have raised the attention of many researchers [32–35]. Proper properties such as high solubility in water, easy synthesis and application, high stability, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility have led to these nanoparticles become widely applicable in numerous fields for example photo catalyst, optical sensors and biosensors and biomedical imaging [36] as better alternatives for organic Dyes and semiconductor quantum dots[37–39].

So far, many efforts have been made to develop carbon dots (CDs) and improve their physico chemical properties and sustainability, and different approaches are used to prepare carbon dots as good as good [40–42]. In most cases, chemical methods are applied for synthesis the CDs rather than physical methods which need to more costly equipment [42–44]. One of the most important goals of green chemistry is to find easy, fast and affordable ways to convert biodegradable wastes to valuable products[45]. In addition, the use of materials having the lowest toxicity is also of particular importance [46,47].

In this study, green CDs were prepared using aqueous and alcoholic extracts of rose flowers with blue, yellow and red pigments under identical synthesis conditions. The optical properties and the stability of CDs were then compared in case of quantum yield, temperature and photo stability. The as prepared Nano carbon dots finally were used as a fluorescence probe to detecting Diazinon and their sensitivity, selectivity, repeatability, and recovery of the method were studied and evaluated (Scheme 1) .

All chemicals used were ofanalytical gradeand wereusedas received without any further operation. Diazinon and other pesticides (abamectin, endosulfan, Brucine sulfate, cyhalothrin, alacholr, imidacloprid, and deltametrin) were purchased from Sigma (St. Louis, MO, USA). Other routine chemicals and solvents were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). The aqueous and alcoholic extracts of blue, red and yellow Rose flowers were prepared for the synthesis of CDs. The stock solution of 1 mM of all pesticides in ethanol was prepared and used for further dilution of deionized water.

Carbon Dot was prepared by hydrothermal treatment of aqueous and alcoholic extracts of rose flowers in different colors. In a routine experiment for the preparation of aqueous and alcoholic extracts, the petals were dried in an oven 60 °C for 30 min and 1.5 g of each dried petal in 50 ml of water or ethanol was used. The extract then filtered and each solution was transferred to an autoclave (80 ml) at 200° C for 2 h. The mixture was then cooled to room temperature and the residuals were removed using filtration as previous step. The final product was freeze dried and stored in 4° C for future use.

The entire fluorescence spectrums were recorded using a Perkin Elmer LS-55 (USA) fluorescence spectrometer. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained with Agilent 8453 (Germany) spectrophotometer. For FTIR experiments, a Shimadzu IR2 Spectrometer (Japan) was used to determine the carbon-dot functional groups. The crystal structure of CDs was studied using X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a Inel Equaniox 3000 [20], 30 kV, 20 mA, K alpha Cu: 1.54 Å. The transmission electron[48] microscope Zeiss EM10C was used to determine the carbon dots size and to determine the fine-graining of the particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Common fluorescence measurements to the detection of Diazinon
ce:section.ce:para:
After studying the physico-chemical properties of the synthesized carbon dots, in order to study their ability for Diazinon determination, the best one was selected in the terms of better optical properties and more stability. After preparing the solution of CDs (10
μ g
mL
)
, the fluorescence spectrum of CDs was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm and emission wavelength of 510 nm. The effect of increasing the amount of pesticides on carbon dots fluorescence intensities was studied in a phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4).

In the next step, the fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured after adding the 3 μL of 10 micro molar of pesticides. Fluorescence spectra were recorded every 5 min after the addition of chemicals. The selectivity of CDs for sensing Diazinon was evaluated against other pesticides containing Abamectin, Endosulfan, and Brucine sulfate, Deltametrin, Cyhalothrin and Imidacloprid under the same conditions. In order to evaluate the applicability and validity, 3 real water samples were used for validating the CD-based sensor for Diazinon.

The CDs were prepared by a simple and rapid hydrothermal method from aqueous and alcoholic extracts of blue, yellow and red roses. Fig. S1, shows the TEM images of the 6 products and the distribution diagram of nanoparticle dispersion, investigate the real microstructure of the synthesized CDs display that the average size of the monodisperse nanoparticles was around 32 nm. The average size of each of the synthesized CDs is given in Table S1. As the images show, the CDs made from alcoholic extracts are more spherical and have a smaller particle size.

FTIR spectrums were obtained to identify the surface functional groups presented on the as-synthesized CDs. As shown in Fig. S2 for both types of synthesized CDs, there are some common groups: it can be noticed that there were two routine peaks at 3394 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1, which were assigned to the stretching and bending vibrations of O–H respectively. The short peak at 2920 cm−1 could be identified as the stretching vibrations of C–H peaks. Respectively, the peaks at 1604 cm−1 and 1076 cm−1 may be related to C = C and C-O and the peak at 1711 cm−1 is related to C = O stretching vibrations in carboxylic acids. In the FTIR results of synthesized CDs from alcoholic extract (Fig S2.B), there are several more peaks observed, for example, the peaks at the 1462 cm−1 and 1168 cm−1, refer to bending vibrations of N–H groups and C-N respectively. The findings show that the CDs prepared by both types of extracts have hydroxyl and carboxyl groups which cause high solubility of these nanoparticles in water.

The crystallinity of CDs were analyzed using the XRD method and results are shown in Fig S3. For CDs prepared by alcoholic extract, broader wide peaks in 2θ = 23, 24 and 25° are found for blue, red and yellow roses (Fig. S3B), which are wider than those obtained for aqueous extracts (Fig. S3A). Possibly, the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups decrease in size of nanoparticles has led to this broadening. In general, for both series of CDs, the X-ray diffraction pattern represents a weak crystalline property, which shows an amorphous carbon phase.

The optical properties of synthesized CDs were investigated using UV–vis and fluorescence spectrums. As shown in Fig. S4, the UV–vis spectrums recorded for carbon dots have two absorbing peaks at 270 nm and 360 nm. The peak located at 360 nm for carbon dots prepared from aqueous extract (Fig. S4A) is weaker than the alcoholic extract CDs (Fig. S4B). Respectively these two peaks can be attributed to the
π →
π
∗
transition of
C = C
bonds and
n →
π
∗
transition of
C = O
bonds in CDs [49].

According to previous reports, the fluorescence spectrums of CDs is depend on their excitation wavelength [50,51]. Accordingly, with an increase in excitation wavelength, the maximum emission shifted and FL intensity also will changed (Fig. S5). For each CD, different wavelengths for excitation were chosen and ultimately the best and strongest emissions were obtained (Fig. S6), and the exhibits strong blue fluorescence at the excitation wavelength of sunlight and λex = 254 nm in Fig.S7.The best excitation wavelength for each of the CDs is shown in Table 1 . As it is shown, the fluorescence of alcoholic extract in larger wavelength than the aqueous extract has been attributed, which may be attributed to the change of dielectric constant of solvent and the presence of more phenolic groups in the alcoholic extract. The quantum yield (Φ) as the ratio of the number of photons emitted to photons absorbed was calculated based on the following equation [52]:
(1)
Φ
CDs
=
Φ S
×
F
CDs
F S
×
A S
A
CDs
where F stands for the integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance, and the subscripts CDs and S stand for carbon dots and standard, respectively. The quantum yield of the standard quinine sulfate (
Φ S
)
is 0.55. The quantum yield is calculated for all CDs and they are shown in (Table 1). The quantum yield show that for all CDs, the yield is acceptable and is approximately same for all CDs which indicate there are no differences between CDs in the case of quantum yield.

The experiments on the fluorescence stability of synthesized CDs were carried out in order to evaluating their withstanding in various conditions. For this purpose fluorescence intensity of CDs toward high ionic strength, various pH values, in time and at different temperatures were evaluated. A 2 M NaCl solution was prepared as a stock solution and used to evaluate the effect of ionic strength on CDs. The results shown in Fig. S8.I indicating that the as synthesized CDs have relatively good stability against high ionic strength and have not changed their fluorescence intensity. However, it should be noted that with increasing ionic strength the intensity is reduced gradually and therefore, it is better that determination should be done at least ionic strength.

Fig. S8.II shows the changes in the emission spectrum of CDs at various pH values. As can be seen the CDs exhibit strong and stable fluorescence intensity in the pH range of 4 to 10. The results indicate good stability of CDs in both high and low values of pH which proofs their stability against protonation [53]. There is some reduction in fluorescence ntensity in concurrent with changes in pH that is not as significant.

Another factor for a CD to be good candidate for sensory applications is its temperature stability. Therefore, the thermal stability were also investigated in the terms of temperature value and period of time the CDs are in exposure to heat. As it is clear, with an increasing in temperature ranging from 17 to 57 °C, the fluorescence intensities are reduced in both aqueous and alcoholic CDs (Fig. S8.III), but the decreasing rate of alcoholic ones is much less than those of aqueous CDs.

In the case of photo stability as shown in Fig. S8.IV the fluorescence intensity did not change significantly after being placed in exposure to ambient light. The results show that over the time of several consecutive days, the FL intensity of CDs were decreased less than 5 percent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of fluorescence response of CDs to Diazinon
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous section and in the case of CD stability, under equal conditions, carbon dots preparing with alcoholic extract of the yellow rose has higher stability and stronger emission than the others. Hence, in the next steps, this CD will be used to study its ability for the detection of Diazinon. Increasing the concentration of Diazinon (5 µM) to CDs will cause a significant change in fluorescence intensity and will result in fluorescence quenching (Fig. S9A). As already explained, the FTIR results show that there are many hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of the CDs can be attributed to the coupling of the Diazinon to the hydroxyl groups of CDs through an effective electron transfer process [50]. In order to obtain the best condition for Diazinon detection, it is necessary to examine the quenching profile of CDs in some effective variables of incubation time and pH.

The effect of the time on the sensitivity of CDs was shown in (Fig. S9B). This experiment shows that the reaction between Diazinon and CDs is about completed after 3 min and after that no more quenching is observed. Accordingly all subsequent experiments are done as the reagents are incubated for 3 min.

The effect of the pH on the CDs and their response to existing of analyst is one of the important factors that should be investigated. Changes in fluorescence intensity of Diazinon applying CDs in different pH (from 4 to 10) are presented in (Fig. S9C). As it is shown, the increase in pH to 7 will increase the response and after pH = 7.0 the response will gradually decrease. Also, at pH = 7.0, there is an acceptable response of CDs to Diazinon. Hence, because of inert condition of neutral pH, this value is used for experiments.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs solution (10.0
μ g
mL
,
λ
ex
=
420 nm) were recorded with increasing concentrations of Diazinon (Fig. 1A) . Fig. 1B shows the linear relationship between variations in fluorescence intensity of CDs and increasing concentrations of Diazinon (0.02–10 µM). The regression equation obtained as:
F 0
- F
F 0
= 0.0324
Q
+ 0.0397
(µM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.97 and a detection limit of 0.01 µM based on S/N = 3 and the relative standard deviation (RSD) for the detection of 0.01 μM of Diazinon (n = 3) is 3.5%.

In order to further investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs by Diazinon (dynamic or static), the Stern-Volmer equation has been used;
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
Q
where the F0 and F are the intensities of free and Diazinon treated CDs, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. Using this equation the KSV related to 3 temperatures of 290, 300 and 310 K were obtained (Table.S1). By increasing temperature the values of Ksv is decreased which is the first sign for static nature of quenching (Fig. S10). The binding constant Kb of Diazinon to CDs is also measured by the following equation:
(3)
log
F 0
- F
F
= log
K b
+ n log
[ Q ]
(4)
Ln
K b
= -
Δ H
RT
+
Δ S
R
(5)
Δ G = Δ H - T Δ S

The values obtained for each of the parameters for the interaction of CDs with Diazinon are also recorded in Table S1. Given the sign of ΔH and ΔS, it can also be predicted that the reaction is reversible. This result also, demonstrates that this binding is impelled by van der Waals interactions and Hydrogen bonds.

An important factor in evaluating the function of any sensor is its selectivity against the goal analyst and this is needed to be investigated. To this end the fluorescence intensity of CDs in the presence of different other pesticides was also checked. Table 2 and represent these results and explain that there is no significant or adverse effects for Diazinon detection related to presence of other chemicals. Also, the tolerance limit for measuring Diazinon in the presence of possible interactions was investigated. In this regard, samples containing a constant amount of Diazinon (0.5 µM) and various additive concentrations of other pesticides were prepared and evaluated. These substances make a relative error of less than ± 5% in results and indicating this method is suitable for the detection of Diazinon (Table 2).

In order to confirm the validity of the proposed method for detection of Diazinon, in real conditions the system was examined using the waste water spiked by fine amount of the pesticide. As indicated in the Table 3 , the recovery of Diazinon in 6 samples is 97.8 to 102.2 which indicate that the as prepared nanosensor have good ability to detect Diazinon in low concentrations.

Today, green synthesis-based nanosensors are widely used in a variety of fields. Features such as easy synthesis, availability, low toxicity, low cost, as well as high accuracy and precision of these nanosensors in various determinations have led researchers to pay special attention to this group of nanoparticles. That's why we used a new method to synthesize green carbon dots to finally use them to determination diazinon.

In this study, the effect of two variables of rose pigments type, and the effect of their extraction methods on optical properties of their synthesized CDs were examined. The results indicated that the prepared CDs of alcoholic extracts with yellow petals have better quantum yields and more stability. In the next step CDs synthesized as a fluorescent probe approach was utilized to Diazinon detection. Both the synthesis method and determination of Diazinon were simple process, green and economic, and the results indicate that the method is very facile and reliable for assessing the level of Diazinon in the real samples as well as laboratory conditions. The comparison of the proposed method with the previous methods used to detection of Diazinon is presented in Table 4 . Results indicate that the linear range and detection limit of the proposed method have the acceptable values in comparison with those data obtained from other approaches.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105232.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of organophosphorus pesticides in water samples by single drop microextraction and gas chromatography-flame photometric detector

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Ahmadi, Y. Assadi, S.M. Hosseini, M. Rezaee, Determination of organophosphorus pesticides in water samples by single drop microextraction and gas chromatography-flame photometric detector, Journal of Chromatography A, 1101 (2006) 307-312.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of diazinon in fruits from northeast of Iran using the QuEChERS sample preparation method and GC/MS

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Akhlaghi, A. Motavalizadehkakhky, R. Emamiyan, Determination of diazinon in fruits from northeast of Iran using the QuEChERS sample preparation method and GC/MS, Asian Journal of Chemistry, 25 (2013) 1727.
I. Dubus, J. Hollis, C. Brown, Pesticides in rainfall in Europe, Environmental pollution, 110 (2000) 331-344.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cloud point extraction for determination of Diazinon: Optimization of the effective parameters using Taguchi method

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R. Sohrabi, S. Jamshidi, A. Esmaeilifar, Cloud point extraction for determination of Diazinon: Optimization of the effective parameters using Taguchi method, Chemometrics and Intelligent Laboratory Systems, 110 (2012) 49-54.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanoparticles in electrochemical sensors for environmental monitoring

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Rassaei, F. Marken, M. Sillanpää, M. Amiri, C.M. Cirtiu, M. Sillanpää, Nanoparticles in electrochemical sensors for environmental monitoring, TrAC Trends in Analytical Chemistry, 30 (2011) 1704-1715.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Exposure to the organophosphate diazinon: data from a human volunteer study with oral and dermal doses

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Garfitt, K. Jones, H. Mason, J. Cocker, Exposure to the organophosphate diazinon: data from a human volunteer study with oral and dermal doses, Toxicology letters, 134 (2002) 105-113.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Initial results of environmental monitoring in the Texas Rio Grande Valley

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Garcia, C. Ake, B. Clement, H. Huebner, K. Donnelly, S. Shalat, Initial results of environmental monitoring in the Texas Rio Grande Valley, Environment international, 26 (2001) 465-474.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Drift from a commercial, aerial application of methyl and ethyl parathion: An estimation of potential human exposure

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.M. Draper, J.C. Street, Drift from a commercial, aerial application of methyl and ethyl parathion: An estimation of potential human exposure, Bulletin of environmental contamination and toxicology, 26 (1981) 530-536.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation, evaluation and application of diazinon imprinted polymers as the sorbent in molecularly imprinted solid-phase extraction and liquid chromatography analysis in cucumber and aqueous samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Davoodi, M. Hassanzadeh-Khayyat, M.A. Rezaei, S.A. Mohajeri, Preparation, evaluation and application of diazinon imprinted polymers as the sorbent in molecularly imprinted solid-phase extraction and liquid chromatography analysis in cucumber and aqueous samples, Food chemistry, 158 (2014) 421-428.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Monitoring of pesticide residues and their metabolites in surface and underground waters of Imathia (N. Greece) by means of solid-phase extraction disks and gas chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Albanis, D. Hela, T. Sakellarides, I. Konstantinou, Monitoring of pesticide residues and their metabolites in surface and underground waters of Imathia (N. Greece) by means of solid-phase extraction disks and gas chromatography, Journal of Chromatography A, 823 (1998) 59-71.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticles-based electrochemical sensor for determination of diazinon pesticide in well water and apple fruit samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Motaharian, F. Motaharian, K. Abnous, M.R.M. Hosseini, M. Hassanzadeh-Khayyat, Molecularly imprinted polymer nanoparticles-based electrochemical sensor for determination of diazinon pesticide in well water and apple fruit samples, Analytical and bioanalytical chemistry, 408 (2016) 6769-6779.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of diazinon at different doses on rat liver and pancreas tissues

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Gokcimen, K. Gulle, H. Demirin, D. Bayram, A. Kocak, I. Altuntas, Effects of diazinon at different doses on rat liver and pancreas tissues, Pesticide biochemistry and physiology, 87 (2007) 103-108.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of diazinon on catalase antioxidant enzyme activity in liver tissue of Rutilus rutilus

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Keramati, S. Jamili, M. Ramin, Effect of diazinon on catalase antioxidant enzyme activity in liver tissue of Rutilus rutilus, Journal of Fisheries and Aquatic Science, 5 (2010) 368-376.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electroanalytical determination of the herbicide picloram in natural waters by square wave voltammetry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R. Massaroppi, S.A. Machado, L.A. Avaca, Electroanalytical determination of the herbicide picloram in natural waters by square wave voltammetry, Journal of the Brazilian Chemical Society, 14 (2003) 113-119.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Whole effluent toxicity of sewage treatment plants in the hawkesbury–nepean watershed, New South Wales, Australia, to Ceriodaphnia dubia and Selenastrum capricornutum

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.C. Bailey, R. Krassoi, J.R. Elphick, A.M. Mulhall, P. Hunt, L. Tedmanson, A. Lovell, Whole effluent toxicity of sewage treatment plants in the hawkesbury–nepean watershed, New South Wales, Australia, to Ceriodaphnia dubia and Selenastrum capricornutum, Environmental toxicology and chemistry, 19 (2000) 72-81.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: U.E.P. Agency, National recommended water quality criteria, Office of Water Washington, DC, 2002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of organophosphorus insecticides (malathion and diazinon) residue in the drinking water

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shayeghi, M. Khoobdel, H. Vatandoost, Determination of organophosphorus insecticides (malathion and diazinon) residue in the drinking water, Pakistan journal of biological sciences: PJBS, 10 (2007) 2900-2904.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry method for the simultaneous quantitative determination of the organophosphorus pesticides dimethoate, fenthion, diazinon and chlorpyrifos in human blood

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Salm, P.J. Taylor, D. Roberts, J. de Silva, Liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry method for the simultaneous quantitative determination of the organophosphorus pesticides dimethoate, fenthion, diazinon and chlorpyrifos in human blood, Journal of Chromatography B, 877 (2009) 568-574.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid simultaneous determination for organophosphorus pesticides in human serum by LC–MS

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Inoue, T. Saito, H. Mase, Y. Suzuki, K. Takazawa, I. Yamamoto, S. Inokuchi, Rapid simultaneous determination for organophosphorus pesticides in human serum by LC–MS, Journal of pharmaceutical and biomedical analysis, 44 (2007) 258-264.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of organophosphorus pesticide residues in Cilento (Campania, Italy) virgin olive oil by capillary gas chromatography

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Rastrelli, K. Totaro, F. De Simone, Determination of organophosphorus pesticide residues in Cilento (Campania, Italy) virgin olive oil by capillary gas chromatography, Food Chemistry, 79 (2002) 303-305.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solid phase microextraction applied to the analysis of organophosphorus insecticides in fruits

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Fytianos, N. Raikos, G. Theodoridis, Z. Velinova, H. Tsoukali, Solid phase microextraction applied to the analysis of organophosphorus insecticides in fruits, Chemosphere, 65 (2006) 2090-2095.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polypyrrole/montmorillonite nanocomposite as a new solid phase microextraction fiber combined with gas chromatography–corona discharge ion mobility spectrometry for the simultaneous determination of diazinon and fenthion organophosphorus pesticides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.T. Jafari, M. Saraji, H. Sherafatmand, Polypyrrole/montmorillonite nanocomposite as a new solid phase microextraction fiber combined with gas chromatography–corona discharge ion mobility spectrometry for the simultaneous determination of diazinon and fenthion organophosphorus pesticides, Analytica chimica acta, 814 (2014) 69-78.
